case_metadata.case_name: B. Sirisena Cooray v. Tissa Dias Bandaranayake and Two Others
legislation_referred: Special Presidential Commissions of Inquiry Law, No. 7 of 1978

headnotes.summary: There was no delay in making the application in that the petitioner made it so soon as he became aware of the finding against him from the report of the Commission after its publication as a sessional paper.

The writ jurisdiction of the Superior Courts is conferred by Article 140 of the Constitution. It cannot be restricted by the provisions of ordinary legislation contained in the ouster clauses enacted in sections 9 (2) and 18A of the SPCI Law or section 22 of the Interpretation Ordinance. In fact the first proviso to section 18A (2) specifically confers writ jurisdiction on the Supreme Court. That jurisdiction is unfettered.

The recommendation or the decision of the Special Presidential Commission has the effect of potentially jeopardising the rights of persons. As such the Commissioner's report is amenable to judicial review. Section 18A (2) of the SPCI Law itself contemplates the exercise of judicial review by the Superior Courts over Commissions appointed under the Law.

The summons issued by the Commission (P5) was flawed as it was not in conformity with sections 7 (1) (c) and 11 (3) of the SPCI Law; Nor was it a notice under section 16. Hence the warrant too was flawed; and the Commission has no power to 'convict' any person of any offence. That power is vested in the Supreme Court - section 10 (1) of the Law.

The determinations and recommendations of the Commission are flawed firstly as being unreasonable in that the Commissioners did not call their own attention to the relevant matters; secondly as they are not based on evidence of any probative value; and thirdly because those determinations and recommendations have been reached without giving the petitioner a right of hearing in breach of the principles of natural justice.

judgment_text.content: The petitioner moved the Court of Appeal by this application for a writ of certiorari to quash the findings, determinations and recommendations, made in respect of him, by a commission which was appointed in terms of the Special Presidential Commissions of Inquiry Law No. 7 of 1978 as amended. The application stood transferred to the Supreme Court in terms of section 18A of the SPCI Law. The warrant issued by the President dated 7th December, 1994, related to the assassination of Lalith William Athulathmudali. The Commission was required to inquire into the circumstances of the assassination, physical attacks on the deceased prior to the assassination, and whether there was a failure by public officers to perform duties or provide security. The Commission found the petitioner responsible for the assassination and recommended civic disability.

At the hearing, counsel for the 1st and 2nd respondents raised legal objections: (a) delay in the petitioner's application; (b) ouster of writ jurisdiction by the SPCI Law and Interpretation Ordinance; (c) the report was not amenable to judicial review. The Court held there was no delay as the petition was filed soon after the report was published as a Sessional Paper.

The Court analyzed whether the writ jurisdiction conferred by Article 140 of the Constitution could be restricted by ordinary legislation (SPCI Law sections 9(2) and 18A, and Interpretation Ordinance section 22). The Court held that the writ jurisdiction is anchored in the Constitution. Citing Wickramabandu v. Herath and other cases, the Court determined that constitutional provisions prevail over ordinary statutory provisions. Section 18A(2) of the SPCI Law itself contemplates judicial review. The jurisdiction conferred by Article 140 is unfettered.

The Court considered whether the Commission's report affects rights and is thus subject to review. Citing The King v. Electricity Commissioners and subsequent case law, the Court moved beyond the strict 'duty to act judicially' to the 'duty to act fairly'. Although the Commission's report is not self-executory, it has the effect of potentially jeopardizing rights (e.g., civic disability, criminal prosecution). Therefore, the Commission has a duty to act fairly, and its report is amenable to judicial review.

The Commission had found the petitioner guilty of contempt for failing to appear. The Court analyzed the difference between a witness (summoned) and a person implicated (informed and entitled to representation). The Court found the document served on the petitioner (P5) was neither a valid summons under section 7 nor a notice under section 16. It was a 'command' not provided for by law. Furthermore, the Commission refused legal representation to the petitioner, violating the SPCI Law. Consequently, the warrant of arrest and the finding of contempt were flawed and made in excess of jurisdiction. The Commission has no power to 'convict' or punish; that power lies with the Supreme Court.

The Commission concluded the petitioner was involved in the conspiracy to assassinate Lalith Athulathmudali based on inferences from his conduct (avoiding the commission) and hearsay evidence (statements regarding U.L. Seneviratne and 'Sotthi Upali'). The Court found these conclusions unreasonable. They were based on 'double hearsay' evidence of a prisoner which lacked probative value. The Commission failed to call its attention to relevant matters and breached principles of natural justice by denying the petitioner a right of hearing through lawyers. The Court rejected the respondents' argument that 'civic disability' is not a punishment and therefore requires a lower standard of proof.

The Court issued a mandate in the nature of a writ of certiorari setting aside and quashing: (1) the determination of contempt; (2) the disqualification of the petitioner as an elector/MP; (3) the finding of complicity in the assassination and corruption; and (4) the recommendation for civic disability. No order was made as to costs.
